questions.prompt: Why does BFS stop guaranteeing shortest paths once graph edges have different weights?

options.text: BFS's guarantee relies on exploring nodes in strict distance order, which only holds when every edge costs exactly one step; with weighted edges, a node reached via more hops can have a lower total cost than one reached via fewer hops, and a plain FIFO queue has no way to represent 'this path is cheaper, revisit.'
BFS cannot be implemented at all on weighted graphs due to compiler limitations.
Weighted graphs are always directed, and BFS only works on undirected graphs.
BFS still works correctly on weighted graphs; this is a common misconception.

questions.explanation: This is the precise mechanism, not a vague 'weights break things' claim: BFS's FIFO queue processes nodes in hop-count order, which coincides with cost order only when all weights are equal. (b) BFS can be run on any graph structurally; it just won't give correct shortest-path answers when weighted. (c) weighted and directed are entirely independent properties of a graph. (d) is false — this is exactly the scenario where BFS's shortest-path guarantee breaks, motivating Dijkstra's algorithm.

questions.prompt: What is the single structural change that turns BFS into Dijkstra's algorithm?

options.text: Replacing the plain FIFO queue with a min-heap (priority queue) ordered by current known distance, so the closest not-yet-finalized node is always expanded next.
Replacing recursion with iteration.
Adding a visited-set, which BFS does not use.
Reversing the direction of all graph edges before traversal.

questions.explanation: This is the chapter's core insight: Dijkstra is BFS with its queue upgraded to a priority queue, needed precisely because weighted edges break BFS's distance-order guarantee under plain FIFO processing. (b) BFS is typically already iterative (queue-based); this isn't the distinguishing change. (c) BFS also uses a visited/seen mechanism (implicitly via the distance array or an explicit visited set) — this isn't unique to Dijkstra. (d) edge reversal is unrelated to the BFS-to-Dijkstra generalization.

questions.prompt: A production routing system has a graph with some negative edge weights (representing rebates/discounts on certain routes) and no negative cycles. A developer applies Dijkstra's algorithm. What happens?

options.text: Dijkstra can silently produce an incorrect shortest-path answer with no error, because its core assumption — a popped node's distance is final once processed — is violated when a later negative edge could retroactively improve an already-finalized distance.
Dijkstra will throw a runtime exception when it encounters a negative edge weight.
Dijkstra still produces correct results as long as there are no negative cycles.
Dijkstra automatically falls back to Bellman-Ford when negative weights are detected.

questions.explanation: This is the chapter's central warning: the failure mode is silent wrongness, not a crash — which is precisely why it's dangerous in production and why knowing this boundary matters more than memorizing pseudocode. Dijkstra has no built-in negative-weight detection or fallback. (b) there's no exception thrown; the algorithm runs to completion and returns a wrong answer. (c) is the specific misconception the chapter corrects — negative weights alone (even without cycles) break the finality assumption; Bellman-Ford, not Dijkstra, is required. (d) no such automatic fallback exists in a standard Dijkstra implementation.

questions.prompt: Why is 'shortest path' undefined in a graph containing a negative-weight cycle?

options.text: A path could traverse the negative cycle repeatedly, reducing its total cost each time with no lower bound, making the 'minimum' cost negative infinity.
Negative cycles make the graph disconnected.
Negative cycles only affect BFS, not shortest-path algorithms in general.
It is defined, just computationally expensive to find, requiring exponential time.

questions.explanation: Each additional loop through a negative cycle strictly decreases total path cost with no floor, so there is no well-defined minimum — this is exactly why Bellman-Ford's guarantee explicitly excludes negative cycles (it can only detect their existence, not produce a finite shortest path through one). (b) cycles (negative or not) don't inherently affect connectivity. (c) this is a fundamental property of the shortest-path problem itself, independent of which algorithm is used. (d) it isn't merely expensive — it's mathematically undefined, no algorithm (however slow) can return a finite correct answer.

questions.prompt: For finding a minimum spanning tree, when should Kruskal's algorithm be preferred over Prim's, and why?

options.text: On sparse graphs, since Kruskal's cost is dominated by sorting E edges (O(E log E)), which is cheap when E is close to V; Prim's heap-based approach pays off more when E approaches V^2 (dense graphs).
Kruskal's should always be preferred regardless of graph density, since it is simpler to reason about.
Prim's cannot be used on graphs with more than 1000 nodes.
Kruskal's is preferred whenever the graph is directed.

questions.explanation: This mirrors the adjacency-list-vs-matrix and BFS-vs-DFS pattern throughout this course: the right choice is a function of graph density, not a universal ranking. Kruskal's edge-sort-dominated cost scales well when edges are few; Prim's heap-growth cost scales better as edges approach the dense extreme. (b) there's no such universal preference — it's density-dependent, which is the whole point of this question. (c) there's no node-count limitation specific to Prim's. (d) MST algorithms are defined for undirected graphs; directedness isn't the deciding factor between Kruskal's and Prim's.

questions.prompt: Kruskal's algorithm sorts all edges by weight, then adds each edge unless it would create a cycle. What data structure efficiently answers 'would adding this edge create a cycle,' and why does that structure fit?

options.text: Union-find (disjoint set) — it tracks which nodes are already connected in near-O(1) per query, so checking whether an edge's two endpoints are already in the same connected component (which would mean adding the edge creates a cycle) is fast and doesn't require re-traversing the growing tree.
A binary search tree, since it can quickly search for whether two nodes are related.
A hash map alone, without any additional structure, since checking membership is already O(1).
A separate BFS traversal of the current tree-so-far for every candidate edge.

questions.explanation: Union-find is purpose-built for exactly this query pattern — 'are these two elements in the same group' with near-constant-time union and find operations (the next chapter covers its mechanics in detail) — which is precisely what cycle detection during MST construction needs, without the overhead of re-scanning the tree built so far. (b) a BST doesn't naturally represent group/connectivity membership in the way this problem needs. (c) a plain hash map has no notion of 'group membership across merges' — it would require re-deriving connectivity from scratch each check. (d) would work correctly but reintroduces O(V+E) cost per edge check, defeating the near-O(1) efficiency union-find provides — exactly the naive alternative this pairing avoids.
